item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The images that were used in this article are non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as the first image could not be found under any source as cc-compliant, and the second image is most likely non-compliant (as supported here). Thus, the images have been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"When recovered SCP-1477 was set to '3'"

On that setting, it would transform lead into gold. Interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, Guildford is in England, on the A3 (which is a highway that passes by it). As a result, "storey" is the proper British spelling. Even the use of the abbreviation is more in keeping with British style, so I think all these issues are resolved by understanding this was written by a British branch of the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this one, but there are some problems that need to be addressed, most of them in the CI incident:

in what had been a first storey bedroom.

Unless this is the British spelling, change to story.

was located on the A3 near Guildford;

Please clarify

been in the employ of the CI

Abbreviations like this shouldn't be used in an official document like this one

elemental gallium

Gallium is my favorite element. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's not a good reason. You should vote on merit, not popularity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this one, but not enough to upvote based on the current number of +votes

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There were a few things that stuck out as being obviously from the UK (I have no idea what the A3 is, presumably a highway of sorts) but other than that I liked it. Upvoted.

@Litfried http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Easy-Bake_Oven

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What's Easy Bake that people keep going on about?

I like it a lot, and I've upvoted, but I will also concur with the unnecessity of the CI bit. Just leave it as the oven which can then break if you want. I'd be happy with it still working, personally.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Easy Bake Alchemy. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Clicked article, saw pink oven. Scrolled down, saw fire. Almost upvoted there.

I'm impressed, upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Easy Bake Alchemy!

I agree with the above, that the incident with the CI is tacked on and unnecessary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Neato.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like how the Foundation try to use it's anomalous affects. Rather than lock it away in horror they put it to work. Such a thing would be incredibly useful in the real world "your own nuclear alchemist in every home!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, I missed that. I think. Or else you changed it and made it clearer. Though now it begs the question, why would a non-anomalous oven become a -1?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The sulphur would have to come from potassium (stepped down by 3, not 2 incidentally), or to have actually been written in a sulphur bearing "ink", possibly sulphur dissolved in a volatile organic solvent, onto the lithium sheet created by transmutation in the oven.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd try again when you get a chance. If you still can't, PM one of the wiki mods.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The title is (or should be) The Philosopher's Stove.

Wikidot wasn't letting me save the page when I went to bed - other people on the IRC said it wasn't working for them either. It's still giving me an error message - is there anyone I should PM? Or just try again later?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's the regular oven they swapped parts with SCP-1477 to see when the anomalous effects kicked in. Is there any way I can make it clearer?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Philosopher's Easy Bake Oven?
Why not?
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a neat idea and it's written well, but it's not really doing anything for me. No vote for now; maybe it'll grow on me later.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very nice science! I agree that the article doesn't need the CI note at the end, but still upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What title? The SCP Series 1000-1999 listing still has ACESS DENIED for this one, and if there's any other way of finding out the title of an SCP I've never heard about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not bad at all, really. My one question is, what exactly is SCP-1477-1? It's not clearly defined.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Obligatory real science upvote.

Also, I like the thin sheet of lithium (which was presumably paper, which is mainly carbon, getting stepped down twice), although I don't know where the sulfur came from.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As I said, it wasn't very clear.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Chaos Insurgency.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What the hell is "the CI"?

EDIT: Oh wait, it's the Chaos Insurgency. Feels a bit tacked on to an otherwise good article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The house in the photo looks North American, or at least like nothing i'd expect to see in Hampshire. A minor nitpick in an otherwise decent entry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It probably should at the very least say "UK" afterward. There's Hampsteads in other places (including Hampstead, New Hampshire, birthplace of the Hampster Dance).

EDIT: Derp. Hampshire, not Hampstead. Still, there's like five Hampshires in the US. Could cause confusion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Probably should be a little more specific. Just as American writers assume everyone should be familiar with America, this is assuming that an international audience has a level of familiarity with the UK that it probably doesn't.

I know that it's a place in Great Britain somewhere. Do I really need to know more?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In the sea without lees
Lies the bird of Hermes
Eating his wings variable
And maketh himself yet full stable.

…when I looked that up and saw the reference to the Staff of Hermes here, I thought it was kind of a clever reference.

So, upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Don't really like the fact you crippled it in the end. Nevertheless I liked this as a draft and I still like it. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding the above. Two agents "going rogue" and stealing a valuable artifact from a secured facility is something that happens on 24, not something that happens in the Foundation-verse.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted for the execution and because the title made me laugh. I have a couple of nitpicks, though:

SCP-1477 was recovered from a surburban residence in north Hampshire

Probably should be a little more specific. Just as American writers assume everyone should be familiar with America, this is assuming that an international audience has a level of familiarity with the UK that it probably doesn't.

I also don't particularly care for the Chaos Insurgency note at the end. It doesn't really add anything to an article that stands pretty nicely on its own.

Overall, though, nice work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A hob's the top cooking surface of a stove, where you put your saucepans - at least in the UK. Looking on Wikipedia it's known as the cooktop or stovetop in the US.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "non-functional hob" did you mean "non-functional knob"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They mostly break apart and form new compounds with ionic bonds (particles within the oven become strongly negatively charged and particles outside positively charged). Any complex hydrocarbons are liable to break down into a chemical soup.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have a question: what happens when the individual elements of a compound are converted into elements which don't bond with each other?
